petitioner_background: The petitioner is a postdoctoral researcher in optical sciences/photonics working at The Pennsylvania State University at the time of filing. He sought classification as an alien of extraordinary ability in the sciences under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act (EB-1A). He was already the beneficiary of an approved employment-based visa petition under section 203(b)(2)(B) (National Interest Waiver). His research focused on white light supercontinuum optical tweezers, coherent anti-Stokes Raman scattering (CARS) spectroscopy, and related biophotonic imaging techniques.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: A certificate confirming a 2007 SPIE Educational Scholarship in Optical Science and Engineering from the Society of Optical Instrumentation Engineers (SPIE), awarded in recognition of the petitioner's 'potential long-range contributions to the field of optics, photonics, and related disciplines.' Counsel asserted SPIE awards scholarships competitively from worldwide applicants. Reference letters from the petitioner's supervisor at Penn State and an associate professor at Tufts University characterized the scholarship as an internationally recognized award. A professor at Penn State also mentioned (without documentation) that the petitioner won another scholarship in 2001 at Nankai University and an Outstanding Doctoral Research Award in Electrical Engineering from Penn State.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the SPIE scholarship did not qualify because it was awarded based on 'potential' future contributions rather than recognition of excellence in the field of endeavor, and academic achievements are not evidence of accomplishments in a field of endeavor (citing NYSDOT). The unsupported assertions of counsel do not constitute evidence (Matter of Obaigbena). The petitioner failed to submit official SPIE materials showing requirements, selection process, number of annual recipients, or media coverage of the selection. Expert letters merely repeated the regulatory language without corroboration. The Nankai scholarship and Outstanding Doctoral Research Award were not in the record — going on record without supporting documentary evidence is insufficient (Matter of Soffici). The regulation also requires prizes and awards in the plural, consistent with the statutory 'extensive evidence' requirement.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of membership in SPIE (Society of Optical Instrumentation Engineers).
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: On appeal, counsel did not contest the director's conclusion that SPIE membership was not qualifying. The record contained no evidence that SPIE requires outstanding achievements of its members as judged by recognized national or international experts. The AAO concurred with the director.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: A June 2005 press release from Penn State reporting on recent work by the petitioner's supervisor, in which the petitioner was named as one of several graduate students who coauthored the underlying article. The press release was reproduced verbatim on three science websites listing the source as Penn State with no specific author identified.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the materials were not 'about' the petitioner relating to his work as required by 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(iii) — rather, they were about a project on which he worked. The regulation requires the author of the published material to be identified, and no specific author was listed. The AAO did not need to reach whether the websites constituted major media because the content was not 'about' the petitioner. The press release focused on the petitioner's supervisor and mentioned the petitioner only as one of several graduate students.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: An October 2008 invitation to review a manuscript for the Journal of Quantum Electronics; April 2009 invitations to review manuscripts for Applied Optics and the Journal of Physics D: Applied Physics; and an invitation to serve as a 'presider' at a CLEO/IQEC session on June 4, 2009 (accepted before the May 14, 2009 filing date, but the session was scheduled after filing).
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The petitioner submitted no evidence that he actually completed any of the manuscript reviews — a mere invitation does not constitute qualifying participation in judging. The 'presider' role had no evidence showing it involved judging the work of participants, and in any event the session occurred after the date of filing, so it could not serve as evidence of eligibility as of that date (citing 8 C.F.R. §§ 103.2(b)(1), (12); Matter of Katigbak). In the final merits determination, the AAO further noted that peer review is routine in the field and not every peer reviewer enjoys international recognition. The Nobel Prize counter-example raised by counsel was unpersuasive because it is the Nobel Prize itself, not the participation in review, that sets the reviewer apart. Without evidence setting the petitioner apart (reviewing for a small elite group of referees, independent requests from a substantial number of journals, or an editorial position at a distinguished journal), the judging evidence was not indicative of national or international acclaim.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Evidence of several articles and conference presentations; citations of petitioner's work in articles including a 2006 Optics Express article (citing a 2004 article as one of four examples re: ultra-bright supercontinuum light applications), a 2008 Optics A Pure and Applied Optics article (citing the 2004 article as one of four examples re: CDCM research), a 2008 Physical Review B article (citing a 2005 article as one of 13 examples), and a 2008 Journal of the Optical Society of America article from the University of Lyon that built upon the petitioner's 2005 Optics Express article. Reference letters from: the petitioner's supervisor describing the lab as the first to develop white light supercontinuum optical tweezers, characterizing the petitioner's work as 'record-breaking' and 'outstanding,' and listing developments including a single-particle-level CARS spectroscopy technique, suppression of nonresonant four-wave mixing background using time-resolved and polarization-discriminated methods, supercontinuum-based WDM confocal imaging, and chromatic two-photon excitation fluorescence imaging; a Penn State professor predicting the 'world's first commercial optical tweezers based on single particle CARS sensing system'; another Penn State professor listing five optical achievements; a third Penn State professor emphasizing citation counts; and three letters from outside Penn State (from a Tufts associate professor/collaborator, a University of Kansas professor, and an MIT associate professor) praising the single-particle-level CARS spectroscopy invention and its potential applications to DNA/tissue/organ detection.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO held that contributions must be both original and of 'major significance,' and major significance requires that results have already been reproduced, confirmed, and applied by other experts. Scholarly articles fall under a separate regulatory category, so publications alone cannot count under this criterion without additional evidence of major significance. As a postdoctoral associate, research and publication were part of the petitioner's entry-level job duties, and not every research project qualifies as 'major significance.' The citation record primarily showed petitioner's work cited as one of several examples rather than as influential breakthroughs — only the University of Lyon article suggested a useful starting point. Reference letters contained bare assertions, vague claims, and speculation about how the work 'could' or 'will' be used rather than concrete examples of current impact. The supervisor's characterization of work as 'record-breaking' was unsupported by examples of use in biophotonic imaging or chemical sensing. The Penn State colleague's prediction of a commercial product did not identify any company developing such a tool. Independent letters did not suggest the authors had personally applied the petitioner's work. The press releases were from the petitioner's own employer, not independent coverage. USCIS may give less weight to opinions that are uncorroborated or merely conclusory (Matter of Caron International, Matter of Soffici). No corroborating evidence existed prior to the preparation of the petition to bolster the letters.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Evidence of several scholarly articles authored by the petitioner appearing in professional or major trade publications or other major media.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner submitted qualifying evidence meeting the plain language requirements of 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(vi). However, in the final merits determination, the AAO noted the record contained evidence of no more than 12 citations of any one article, and most citations merely cited the petitioner's work as one of several examples of other work in the field. The publication record was not indicative of sustained national or international acclaim.

outcome_reasoning: The petitioner submitted qualifying evidence under only one of the ten regulatory categories (scholarly articles), failing to meet the minimum requirement of three categories. In the final merits determination, the AAO concluded the evidence in the aggregate did not distinguish the petitioner as one of the small percentage who has risen to the very top of the field of endeavor. The petitioner, a postdoctoral associate (an inherently entry-level position preceding faculty appointment), relied on an educational scholarship based on potential, volunteer participation in the widespread peer review process, press releases from his own employer, his publication record, and praise from peers. The AAO contrasted him with his supervisor and letter-writers who had CAREER awards, fellow memberships, elected fellowships of SPIE and OSA, and associate editor positions — showing the true top of the field was far above the petitioner's level. The petitioner failed to establish sustained national or international acclaim, and the appeal was dismissed.
